data.title: Nvidia: Despite Risks, New Signals Of Potential Upside Ahead
data.description:
With AI stocks experiencing dramatic price increases (see table below) there is increasing fear of an AI bubble (that could soon burst). However, considering the five new signals of growth for leading AI chipmaker, Nvidia (NASDAQ:NVDA), the risks of a bubble seem overblown. After reviewing the new signals and risks, as well as Nvidia’s current valuation, I offer my opinion about investing in Nvidia and the “AI bubble.”

Nvidia Overview

Unless you’ve been living under a rock, you know Nvidia is the leading AI Graphics Processing Unit (“GPU”) chipmaker, and the shares have experienced truly breathtaking growth in recent years (see table above) as the company fuels the AI and cloud datacenter megatrend. Revenue growth has been tremendous (see below), and Nvidia is now the largest US stock by market cap at over $4 trillion.

However, considering a lot of people are increasingly worried about Nvidia and AI bubbles, here are five new developments signaling continuing strong growth.

1. China Concerns Overblown

Perhaps the biggest concern for many Nvidia pundits is President Trump’s so-called trade war and its negative impacts on US-China trade in particular. For example, just this past week, China’s market regulators accused NVIDIA of violating anti-monopoly laws (related to its 2020 Mellanox acquisition), and thereby escalating US-China trade tensions. However, Nvidia is already forecasting zero China revenues this quarter, which may prove an overly conservative estimate considering a recent US trade deal does allow H20 chips to be sold in China.

Further, Nvidia relies less and less on China for revenue anyway, as you can see in the following Wall Street Journal chart.

As such, Nvidia-China fears may be overblown.

2. UK Footprint Expanding

Nvidia recently announced significant investment in the UK’s AI ecosystem. For example, Nvidia is partnering with CoreWeave (CRWV), Microsoft (MSFT), and cloud platform Nscale to build advanced AI infrastructure using Blackwell Ultra GPUs. Also, the Stargate UK project with OpenAI aims to deploy GPT-5, and importantly signals Nvidia’s (and AI’s) strategic expansion in Europe. This is a good sign for continuing growth.

3. Trump Is Supporting Nvidia and AI

According to Nvidia founder and CEO, Jensen Huang, President Trump has big plans to support AI and Nvidia in particular. And that includes also the President’s plan for AI energy dominance (more on this later). And just this past week, a massive new deal was announced for Nvidia to partner with US chip maker Intel (INTC) (via a $5B investment). Nvidia basically ate Intel’s datacenter lunch over the last decade (GPUs are better than CPUs in datacenters), and the push to work together is a good sign that the need for AI (and quality semiconductor chips) has more long-term growth ahead.

4. Nvidia SK Hynix Partnership looking up

A lot of investors realize the demand for Nvidia AI chips has been insatiable (a good thing for Nvidia’s pricing power). However, many of them wrongly believe the supply of Nvidia chips is limited by Taiwan Semiconductor (TSM) (the foundry that produces Nvidia chips) or by ASML (ASML) (the company that builds the machines that TSM uses to produce the chips). In reality, neither TSM or ASML is the bottleneck, but rather it is SK Hynix (HXSCF) (the Korean company and Nvidia’s primary provider of High Bandwidth Memory, or “HBM,” which requires costly advanced machines for precise “chiplet stacking,” and this has limited global supply).

The good news is that SK Hynix just announced they completed development of the more efficient HBM4 (i.e. the next-generation HBM for AI applications) and it is ready for mass production. For perspective, SK Hynix is sold out through 2025 on HBM3E (for Nvidia Blackwell chips), but the company’s partnership with Nvidia, including exclusive deals, positions Nvidia for strong growth as they address HBM shortages in the face of insatiable AI chip hunger. This is good news for Nvidia, especially considering the HB4s have 40% improved power efficiency compared to HBM3E (as energy use is the next big constraint on AI growth).

5. Surging AI Energy Demand Signals Growth

According to the International Energy Agency, global electricity demand from datacenters is projected to more than double by 2030, reaching around 945 terawatt-hours (roughly equivalent to Japan's current total power usage). Further, Deloitte forecasts that AI-specific datacenter power needs could grow 30-fold by 2035 (basically fueled by hyperscalers like Microsoft and Alphabet (GOOGL)). In the US, datacenters already consume over 4% of electricity, potentially rising to 12% by 2028 (and thereby pushing utilities and enterprising innovators towards nuclear and increasingly efficient infrastructure solutions).

Surging energy demands for datacenter AI is a good signal for Nvidia and the AI megatrend in general (but it also creates new challenges and opportunities for investment, as I wrote about here).

Valuation Fear

Just because a stock price goes up a lot, that does NOT mean the shares are overvalued. Rather, from a valuation standpoint (as you can see in the earlier table) Nvidia trades at very reasonable valuation multiples (price compared to earnings, sales and growth) as compared to other leading megacaps. For example, at 27.9x forward earnings, Nvidia is reasonable compared to Microsoft at 28.5x and Meta at 26.0x, but when you factor in Nvidia's higher expected sales growth and earnings growth rates (see earlier table), the shares become quite attractive. Specifically, Nvidia's low 0.6x forward PEG ratio (price-to-earnings-to-growth) markes it more attractive than all of its megacap peers in this regard. Furthermore, Oracle’s (ORCL) Larry Ellison recently emphasized Nvidia’s GPUs as critical to its AI infrastructure, and Oracle’s new $4B deal with OpenAI (powered by Nvidia chips) underscores the sustained demand for Nvidia GPUs.

Risks

In addition to trade wars (which appear overblown) and valuation concerns (which also seem unwarranted), Nvidia also faces risks from competition (as hyperscalers and other companies work to create their own AI chips), from the threat of new (more efficient) AI models which require less compute (China’s DeepSeek already spooked the market once), new computer technologies (e.g. quantum computing), and the simple risk of overbuilding (this is why I assume hyperscalers outsource to companies like CoreWeave (CRWV), Nebius (NBIS) and Applied Digital (APLD) instead of just buying them), none of these risks appear overly ominous on the horizon just yet. Nvidia still appears to have plenty of room to run.

Conclusion

AI is a megatrend, Nvidia is the premier leader, and both are showing signals of continued strong growth. In my opinion, rather than thinking about selling Nvidia (I continue to personally own a large position), investors should be considering new opportunities to invest in the companies that will benefit from (and grow with) Nvidia, such as dramatic AI-energy needs (for datacenters) in the coming years.

However, at the end of the day, you need to do what is right for you, based on your own individual situation. Disciplined goal-focused long-term investing continues to be a winning strategy (and Nvidia and AI continue to be a big part of mine).

data.title: Morgan Stanley: Rate Cut Creates Opportunities In The Fixed Income Segment
data.description:
Introduction

Morgan Stanley (NYSE:MS) is one of the largest US financial institutions. As it enjoys a worldwide brand recognition and reputation, it's one of the few companies that doesn’t need a lengthy introduction. In this article I’ll explain why I think the common shares have gotten a bit too expensive, but also why I think the preferred equity could still be interesting. In my personal portfolio, I have sold my entire position in Morgan Stanley’s common shares, but some of the preferred shares have an increased likelihood of getting called in the next few years, resulting in a rather attractive risk/reward ratio.

Morgan Stanley’s net profit remains strong, thanks to the trading division

As this article will focus on a specific series of preferred shares where I anticipate a call in two years from now, the discussion of Morgan Stanley’s financial results will focus on the bank’s ability to pay the preferred dividends.

As the income statement below shows, Morgan Stanley reported total non-interest income of $14.45B, an increase of in excess of 10% compared to the second quarter of 2024, mainly thanks to a robust performance in the trading division. The higher investment income and commission and fee income obviously also help (but this of course also goes hand in hand with higher G&A expenses).

Ass you can see above, Morgan Stanley also reported a 13.5% increase in the net interest income, which increased to $2.35B. Unfortunately, this goes hand in hand with a higher loan loss provision, but with $196M in quarterly provisions (bringing the total amount of provisions in the first half of the year to $331M), Morgan Stanley can of course easily absorb these provisions.

In fact, considering the pre-tax income was $4.62B and the pre-tax and pre-provision profit was approximately $4.8B, which means that even if the loan loss provisions increase by a factor of 20 (which I of course don’t think is very likely), Morgan Stanley would remain profitable.

Going back to the bank’s reported earnings, the net profit of $3.58B contained about $36M attributable to non-controlling interests, resulting in a net profit of $3.54B, of which $147M was required to cover the preferred dividends. This indeed means Morgan Stanley needed to use less than 5% of its net income to cover these preferred dividends. Meanwhile, the bottom line result was $3.4B which works out to an EPS of $2.15. The EPS in the first half of the year was approximately $4.78 per share.

The rate cut makes a call of the Series P preferred shares more likely

I discussed the Series P (NYSE:MS.PR.P) preferred shares after the initial offering, and it goes without saying these fixed-rate preferred shares offer excellent exposure to fixed income from Morgan Stanley, with an above average call risk. After all, the lower the interest rates on the financial markets, the more "expensive" these preferred dividends are for Morgan Stanley and the higher the likelihood these preferred shares will be called.

Seeing how the preferred shares with a low fixed coupon are trading (just to give you an idea, the Series L and Series O offer a current yield of approximately 5.5-5.6% with a lower likelihood of being called due to the low absolute preferred dividend payments), it's increasingly likely Morgan Stanley may issue a security with a lower preferred dividend ahead of the October 2027 call date of the Series P preferred shares to retire the latter. With $1B in Series P outstanding, cutting the preferred dividend by 125 bps to 5.25% (which is not unlikely, as I expect a few more rate cuts in the next two years) would save Morgan Stanley $12.5M per year. This would add almost a cent to the EPS on the common shares.

As you can see above, the Series P preferred shares closed at $25.88 on Wednesday, which means they are trading at approximately 3.5% above par. This results in a current yield of 6.3%.

However, given the increasing likelihood of a call in 2027, I think it makes more sense to look at the yield to call and total return vs. the current yield. Between today and the call date on Oct. 15 in 2027, the preferred shareholders will receive nine more quarterly preferred dividend payments of $0.40625, resulting in $3.65 (rounded down) in total preferred dividend payments. This means that anyone buying the stock now at $25.88 will receive $28.65 over the course of the next 25 months. This results in a total return of $28.65 / $25.88 = 10.7% between now and Oct. 15, 2027.

This results in a yield to call of approximately 5.3%. While that's lower than buying the preferred stock with a lower call risk, this yield to call still represents a mark-up of 172 bps to the two-year Treasury yield (which currently stands at 3.58%). And in the (I think unlikely) event the securities only get called later on, the yield to call increases. A call about one year after the first call date would push the yield to call to approximately 5.5%, in line with the other preferred securities that are currently outstanding.

Investment thesis

I don’t like to slap a "sell" on a company, but I think Morgan Stanley’s common shares are ‘more than fairly valued’ right now as the stock is trading at 15-17 times earnings. Together with JPMorgan (JPM), Morgan Stanley is one of my favorite large financial institutions, but I think this is an excellent moment to monetize my position in the common shares and recycle the cash into other opportunities. As you may have noticed by now, I have been having a closer look at some regional and local banks, and I still see some opportunities there.

I still think the preferred equity of Morgan Stanley is a "buy," despite seeing a lower preferred yield of around 5.5-5.6%. And I also think the Series P offer an attractive risk/reward ratio even if you’d assume that specific series will get called in 2027.

data.title: Undercovered Dozen: Applied Digital, Merck, B2Gold And More
data.description:
The Undercovered Dozen is a weekly Seeking Alpha editor-curated series highlighting 12 articles on lesser-covered stocks from the previous seven days. We hope this provides ideas and inspires discussion among the community.

Today we're looking at articles published between Sep. 12 and Sep. 18.

Take a look at what these less-covered stocks might hold for you. And please join the conversation below to share what you think: Are any of these worth following up on? And are there other undercovered ideas that you like?

Buy B2Gold: The Bullish Rally Is Just Beginning

Dmytro Lebid | Buy

The current environment in the gold market remains highly positive, with prices hitting new all-time highs. This week, we saw gold reach $3,647 per ounce, a new record that shows no signs of slowing down and is poised to climb higher. The price surge is driven by a combination of factors, including mounting global and U.S. recession fears and expectations of increased liquidity in financial markets.

A price of $4,000 per ounce is no longer a prospect for 2026 but is now a potential target by the end of 2025. Accordingly, these developments have created a strong bullish trend for gold mining stocks.

B2Gold (BTG), in particular, stands out with a high Quant Rating of 4.92 points. While the company ranks ninth out of 47 in the gold mining sector, its impressive and diversified asset portfolio enables it to generate a stable operating cash flow.

Continue reading here...


Forget Enbridge: Here's Why TC Energy Is The Better Choice Today

Samuel Smith | Hold

Enbridge (ENB) and TC Energy (TRP) are two leading Canadian midstream infrastructure giants. In addition to avoiding the K-1 tax form that many investors dread, these two companies offer investors a very attractive combination of yield, safety, and growth potential, as well as significant exposure to the AI boom, all in one package.

In this article, I will compare them side by side and share why I believe TRP currently gets the edge over ENB.

Continue reading here...


Merck: Buy This Dividend Powerhouse While It's Cheap

Gen Alpha | Strong Buy

Many income stocks remain beaten down in price, especially those in the real estate and pharmaceutical sectors. This is despite talk of potential interest rate cuts in the near future, which could make dividend stocks more appealing compared to money market funds. Yet the market operates on its own schedule, and that’s a great thing for income investors looking to layer into quality dividend stocks at bargain valuations.

This brings me to Merck & Co., Inc. (MRK), which I last covered in January, highlighting its robust drug pipeline and strong balance sheet. Merck continues to trade in value territory at the current price of $81, sitting close to its 52-week low. ...

In this article, I revisit MRK, including recent business results, and discuss why it’s currently a bargain for value, income, and growth for long-term investors, so let’s get started!

Continue reading here...


ULTY: How YieldMax Reduced NAV Erosion While Sustaining 80%+ Yields

Kevin Shan | Buy

When the YieldMax Ultra Option Income Strategy ETF (ULTY) first launched in February 2024, I was immediately skeptical of the fund. At the time, it boasted a yield of over 100%, and its strategy involved running synthetic covered call strategies on the most volatile assets they could trade on. My own prediction was that the fund's NAV would be quickly eroded as these volatile assets were also risky stocks that often had extreme downsides. In other words, the fund may have been able to generate enormous payouts, but the loss in the underlying would have far exceeded any premiums generated. And I was right. ULTY's share price had fallen by over 60% from its inception to March 2025, and distributions were not enough to create overall positive total returns.

In March 2025, however, YieldMax decided to make changes that suddenly propelled ULTY from a couple hundred million dollars in assets under management ("AUM") to over three billion.

The most obvious change was ULTY's switch to weekly distributions, but this was only a small part of a bigger story. YieldMax had actually modified its options strategy on ULTY and created a fund that has since performed much better. NAV erosion has been minimized, and yet, the fund still yields 87.42% based on its most recent distribution. As YieldMax's Jay Pestrichelli puts it in an interview with Marcos Milla at 2:20, "I've always thought ULTY was our closest thing to a hedge fund." And while this "hedge fund" has not proven itself in down markets yet, the continual successful adaptations from YieldMax have compelled me to rate ULTY as a small buy. The fund can work well in small allocations next to other income assets, especially in cases to help generate a higher blended yield.

Continue reading here...


JEPI: Left Behind Again

Stone Fox Capital | Sell

The JPMorgan Equity Premium Income ETF (JEPI) has again failed to keep up with a strong market rally. The covered call ETF continues to provide solid income, but the returns constantly fail to keep up with the benchmark S&P 500. My investment thesis is still bearish on the struggling ETF that fails to keep up with market returns while still not fully protecting investors from downside risk.

Continue reading here...


SPYI: This Covered Call ETF Is Not What You Think It Is - It's Worse

Sungarden Investment Publishing | Sell

Overrated. It is the single word I've used to describe covered call ETFs as an asset class for several years. And I'm not backing down now. Because the stakes are too high in this juiced-up, liquidity-chasing market. And, because many people, perhaps millions, in my age demographic (borderline baby boomer/Gen-X) are still operating on blind faith. Or just simple lack of information and analysis that goes beyond sales pitches.

So, let's take a time-out. First I'll define some terms so we're all on the same page. So we can save some time for all of us, as I anticipate scanning through the comments and answering those who don't get my nuanced but consistent take on these very successful yet very misleading ETFs. Which, by the way, have become the closest thing the ETF industry has to meme stocks.

This article aims to convey the key takeaways, which really are warnings to investors. Because the worst feeling one can have as an investor is realizing after the fact, when it is too late, that they were either under-informed, misinformed, or complacent about something. And it just took away a chunk of their hard-earned wealth. That's the former investment advisor/fiduciary in me talking there.

OK, here's some quick Q&A that applies to my opinions on covered call ETFs in general. Then I'll focus specifically on how those general aspects of my research are applied to the strong-performing but overrated Neos S&P 500(R) High Income ETF (SPYI).

Continue reading here...


IGLD: Get The Best Of Both Worlds, A Unique Blend Of Safety And Monthly Income

The Dividend Collectuh | Buy

Gold has long been seen as a safe haven for investors. And with economic uncertainty remaining high due to tariffs, the direction of interest rates, and potential economic weakening, the price of gold has skyrocketed in recent years.

Gold futures recently set a new record, up close to 40% in the past year. This could be a signal that investors still remain uncertain about the U.S. economy and that we may be headed towards a period of lower growth.

Stagflation perhaps? Interest rates are set to decline this week as the weak August jobs report has seemingly paved the way for multiple rate cuts this year.

However, I think it will be a while before the economy gains clarity, leaving the door open for gold futures to continue to see new highs. One way to get on board the train is by investing in equities with exposure to the rare metal.

Even better, buying those that pay you monthly income, like the FT Vest Gold Strategy Target Income ETF (IGLD), gives investors, especially retirees, the best of both worlds: safety & income.

Continue reading here...


GPIQ Is The Clear Outperformer Over JEPQ Using Multiple Criteria

Skeptical12 | Sell

The idea that investors must choose between income and total returns in 2025 has clearly become a false narrative. Evolution is part of life and also an important aspect of finance. Today, there are a number of appealing options that individuals can choose from that offer a solid balance between income and total returns.

One of the increasingly common forms of investment for those prioritizing consistent distributions is covered-call funds. Some of the more basic options-based investing options, such as the Global X funds, were the first covered-call funds to gain widespread interest, but these ETFs used very simple approaches that have not held up over time. Many of the newer covered call funds are using much more nuanced and effective strategies to offer investors a better balance between income and total returns.

Two of the newer covered-call funds are the JPMorgan Nasdaq Equity Premium Income ETF (JEPQ) and the Goldman Sachs Nasdaq-100 Premium Income ETF (GPIQ). JEPQ's inception date was May 2022, while GPIQ's inception date was more recent, in October 2023. Both of these funds seek to track an index of stocks very close to the Nasdaq-100.

Continue reading here...


Applied Digital: I'm Eyeing Another AI-Related Data Center Deal

The Techie | Buy

We saw multiple data center stocks fly last week on the back of news that Nebius Group N.V. (NBIS) and Microsoft Corporation (MSFT) have come to a whopping deal for the lease of Nebius’ Vinland data center. Applied Digital Corporation (APLD) was fortunate enough to fall within that peer group and caught some nice tailwind in the Tuesday trading session. The stock is up 36% over the last 5 days.

The key here is that Applied Digital's data centers, just like Nebius', are tailored toward AI. The market moving forward is clearly bullish on those who are ready to go into AI-oriented centers built from the ground up. In my previous coverage of Applied Digital, I mentioned how they should face some nice tailwinds purely from the fact that their centers are built for AI use, while we're seeing everyone else scrambling to retrofit their existing sites. I'm up roughly 14% since that call. Looking forward, the company still has a lot of real estate to expand and capitalize on. Their 400MW campus at Polaris Forge 1 is fully contracted to CoreWeave, Inc. (CRWV) for the next 15 years; however, Applied Digital still has an additional 600MW expansion room at the site with plans to scale up to 1GW.

That's not all, for their Polaris Forge 2 site, management is already in discussion with U.S.-based hyperscalers to potentially make a deal for that capacity. After the latest surge, shares for Applied Digital shares are now in the $18 range. For those who have held shares until this point, I don't see any harm in trimming profits since the stock is up 166% over the past 6 months and has heavily outperformed the S&P 500 (SP500).

Continue reading here...


Occidental Petroleum: Ready For A Downturn, Positioned For A Rally

IWA Research | Strong Buy

I called Occidental Petroleum (OXY) a very solid and undervalued stock back in my previous article titled Occidental Petroleum: There's Big Value In One Of The Best Oil Stocks In The U.S., being backed by good and improving fundamentals, low break-even costs, and strong cash flows that can support great shareholder returns, being one of the best oil stocks in the US in the current market, at least when talking about larger players.

I’d like to reiterate and even upgrade my position in this article, as the company and the industry have seen some developments that are worth mentioning.

Continue reading here...


Structural Changes In Fertilizer Markets Make CVR Partners An Incredible Value

Publius | Strong Buy

CVR Partners (UAN) is a master limited partnership (MLP), engaged in the production of nitrogen fertilizers in the heartland of the Midwest United States. Most of its sales are of urea ammonium nitrate ("UAN"), with the balance being ammonia. They sell a very small amount of urea, DEF, nitric acid, and carbon dioxide, but not really enough to move the needle. Because the stock symbol and the product they sell are both "UAN," I will use CVRP to describe the partnership and UAN to describe the fertilizer. Also, CVR Partners is not to be confused with CVR Energy (CVI), even though CVRP is 37% owned by CVR Energy and is included in their financials. Its primary competitors are CF Industries (CF), Koch Industries, and Nutrien (NTR).

CVR owns two production facilities. Its original plant is located in Coffeyville, Kansas, and the other in East Dubuque, Illinois. Also, since this is a partnership, the equity is units, not shares of stock, and cash payments made are distributions, not dividends. There are significant tax differences between Master Limited Partnership distributions and stock dividends, as well as upon the ownership and sale of MLPs. I strongly encourage readers to understand these differences before buying any MLP.

To get a better understanding of the business, the markets that they operate in, and the partnership itself, I highly recommend reading some of my previous articles on CVRP. In my last article, I focused on the likelihood of CVRP being bought out. While I continue to believe that that will eventually happen, today I would like to go back to basic fundamentals of forward earnings and discuss how different the fertilizer markets are today than they have been in the past.

Continue reading here...


Stride: The Hidden Educational Infrastructure Play Trading At A GARP Sweet Spot

Pau Galindo Ortigosa | Buy

When Stride (LRN) crossed pandemic highs and set up another all-time high above $170, the market had fundamentally misunderstood the structural transformation of this education technology giant. As investors focus on post-COVID normalization and K-12 enrollment volatility, they are missing the more important narrative: the "Educational Infrastructure Transition." In simple words, with steady pricing at pre-pandemic levels, educational institutions are now adopting Stride's technology backbone, which can trigger a recurring revenue stream with sustainable pricing power and market expansion over the next 3-5 years, supported by 80%+ gross margins.

This isn't just another online education recovery story. Stride is undergoing a fundamental change in the way education is delivered across America. From its K-12 virtual schools to its Career Learning division seeing a 35% growth year over year, workforce programs are rapidly evolving. Stride is the very definition of Growth at a Reasonable Price with its PEG ratio at 0.76, forward P/E at 17.3x, and EBITDA growth at 28.6%. The technical setup supports this thesis, with the stock consolidating at critical resistance levels around the $160 mark after nearly a decade of strong gains. Stride is a Buy with a 12-month price target of $210.

data.title: Eaton: The Pulse Of Electrification; Buy
data.description:
Executive Summary

I have been thinking about the AI megatrend for the past few weeks and was wondering how investors can play this trend safely. Eaton (NYSE:ETN) grabbed my attention here. Whether one believes in the AI mega trend or not, the fact of the matter is that hyperscalers continue to invest and grow capex. Reminds me of John Hammond's famous quote from Jurassic Park: "We spared no expense"!

Anyway, back to the topic, I think the demand for AI infrastructure will remain resilient and grow. One could invest in NVIDIA (NVDA) to play this trend, but is everyone comfortable holding NVDA? (Full disclosure: I do hold NVDA, and it's a small portion of my portfolio). So, I shifted my focus to what enables NVIDIA chips to do what they can.

Electrification is the need of the hour. We need a massive amount of energy to run these AI data centers. One could invest in utilities, but I'll be frank - where's the fun in that? So, I started looking for other beneficiaries with this electrification boom, and there it was: Eaton was the center of this boom.

I will explore all of Eaton's business segments and its growth potential in subsequent sections. My DCF analysis suggests a fair value of around $380-$400, and that too being conservative in my assumptions. The company faces multi-year tailwinds and has excellent profitability and momentum ratings based on Seeking Alpha's quant ratings.

Thus, I initiate my coverage with a Buy rating!

Investment Thesis - A Detailed Look Into the Segments

Electrical Americas

Electrification

Experts estimate that the electricity demand in the U.S. will grow at a 2.5% CAGR through 2035 as compared to a 0.5% CAGR between 2014 and 2024. This was highlighted by the CEO as well during the 2025 Laguna Conference.

At the end of the day, we only have so much electricity, and it's a fight to secure this resource. Every company out there wants to move fast (in terms of product development, completion of projects, etc.), and Eaton enables them to do exactly that.

Data Centers

During the 2024 Annual Laguna Conference, the data center backlog was highlighted to be $150 billion. In the 2025 Annual Laguna Conference, it was mentioned that this backlog has now increased to $470 billion, a whopping 213% increase. This gives me confidence that the AI boom has more room to run.

Grey Space

In the data center, grey space refers to the infrastructure, power distribution, cooling systems, etc., that are crucial for running the center. Eaton is an industry expert when it comes to gray space. Now, the question here is who is ahead in this AI data center race. Every company wants to build and deploy these data centers as soon as possible. Traditionally, these data center enclosures are built on-site and are subject to delays such as weather and take a long time for completion.

Fibrebond (a recent Eaton acquisition) builds modular enclosures (comes in precast concrete, interlocking steel, or a hybrid of both). These can be deployed on-site in a very short time.

White Space

This relates to the equipment, such as racks, networking gear, and chips. With the advent of AI, the power requirement and sophistication of the white space have increased. Companies need to focus on running the white space as efficiently as possible to maintain costs. Eaton's Brightlayer data center suite is exactly meant to help companies monitor their grey and white space and ensure the data center is operating efficiently. With the advent of AI, the white space business will now become lucrative for Eaton.

Similarly, the company entered into a partnership with Nvidia in Q2 to help develop and power next-generation AI data center infrastructure. In my opinion, this will be the key catalyst for the company's growth.

HVDC

The current generation of data centers uses alternating current (AC) for all power distribution from the grid to the racks in the data center. The AC voltage from the grid (like 13000+ volts) is stepped down to 54V direct current (DC) for power supply to these racks (kilowatt-scale power consumption). But with the advent of AI, data centers will soon be equipped with megawatt scales for these racks. Apparently, if we use this traditional power distribution solution (i.e., use the 54 VDC), then it would require us 200 kg of copper busbar. Why?

Please bear with me as I revisit high school science here.

Power = Voltage x Current

We know our data center AI power requirement - 1 megawatt (1,000,000 watts).

So to deliver 1 megawatt of power at 54 V, we need (1,000,000 / 54) amps - i.e., 18,518 amps.

This is huge and thus requires a lot of copper (in terms of the thickness of the wires for power distribution).

This is where HVDC comes into the picture.

Nvidia is working on an industrial revolution here to switch from this traditional architecture to HVDC (High Voltage Direct Current). Nvidia wants to use about 800 volts DC from the grid to the edge of the data center. Using the same formula above, to deliver 1 megawatt of power at 800 V, we need (1,000,000 / 800) amps - i.e., 1,250 amps. Much less than the 54 V scenario.

There are other advantages to using high voltage for transmission, such as efficiency, cost savings, future-proofing for electricity needs in the future, etc. This switch is not an easy task, and that's why Nvidia is partnering with power distribution leaders such as Eaton to collaborate on this and develop next-gen data centers.

Cloud

Although Eaton's AI-related orders increased from 15% to 30% this year. So 70% of its business is still cloud, and this should support this segment in case of a slowdown in AI spending from hyperscalers. These themes have given me the confidence that the growth rate for this segment will stay elevated above 13% in the years to come.

The below screenshot is my quarterly revenue build for this segment:

The below screenshot is my annual revenue build for this segment:

Electrical Global

This segment's margins are much lower than the Electrical Americas segment. Europe is a much weaker market than the U.S.; however, the company has brought in a new team (from the Electrical Americas division) to lead this segment. The plan is to leverage the know-how and strive to improve margins. It will always be a challenge for a team to switch from the U.S. to Europe, as these are culturally distinct and would require tremendous effort. But I believe in the management to execute this properly.

A recent report articulated that Europe's data center market is projected to receive about $114 billion in investments in the next 5 years. The goal is to focus on sustainability and efficient power transmission - that's Eaton's backyard. I believe this segment will soon catch up with the Americas segment in terms of operating margins. Currently, the margins for this segment are around 20%, and I believe in a few years this can attain margins around 25%. However, I have been skeptical of a possible (quick) recovery in the European markets. There's too much uncertainty still in this market, and thus I am projecting margin accruement to about 22.5% by the end of 2030.

In addition, Eaton has started investing in the Middle East, anticipating growing data center trends there. The management has not included this tailwind in its already strong forecasts.

Based on the above thesis, I have constructed my quarterly revenue build for this segment:

The below screenshot is my annual revenue build for this segment:

Vehicle

This is the third segment for Eaton, where the company provides power management solutions for the auto industry. This segment has been very weak in recent quarters. This shouldn't be a surprise, as we all know how weak the auto industry is. But I believe things could be turning around here soon. If you look at the (actual) quarterly revenue build as shown below, you will notice that the last quarter's growth rate improved. We got a reversal, although still negative for Q2 2025.

In addition, a recent S&P Global article indicated strong July and August auto sales. Investors should watch for possible recovery in the auto industry. Recovery could help margins for not only Eaton but also other industrials that have exposure to this industry. Nevertheless, I remain pessimistic here and project a ramp in revenue growth decline in Q3 and Q4 to -9.2% and -11.1%, respectively. I project a recovery (positive growth) by 2028 for this segment.

The below screenshot is my quarterly revenue build for this segment:

The below screenshot is my annual revenue build for this segment:

eMobility

This segment ties to the electric vehicle industry. With how things are currently in the U.S., I do not expect any meaningful growth for this segment. As this is also the smallest segment, I assumed a constant revenue growth for this segment YoY. This is because I expect the company to implement cost savings and efficient improvements for this segment at a minimum. The below screenshot is my quarterly revenue build for this segment:

The below screenshot is my annual revenue build for this segment:

Total Revenue Growth

Based on all the above analysis and assumptions, the below are my 5-year revenue and margin projections:

Fundamental Analysis

If you are into numbers like me, feel free to read through my DCF analysis. If you are short on time, feel free to skip to the end of the section, where I provide the sensitivity analysis.

Income Statement Forecast

Using the above revenue growth rates, I constructed the three-statement model as depicted in this section. You will notice that my gross profit margins are in the 38% range. I am being conservative here. Eaton will be a huge beneficiary in this AI data center growth story here, and the company has room to expand margins. However, I want to remain conservative here so as not to inflate my DCF outputs.

Discounted Cash Flow Valuation

I have summarized my DCF calculations in the graphic below. Please comment if you have any questions about this.

WACC

I always highlight in all my articles that the WACC is the most controversial section, and small changes in the WACC can have a huge impact on valuations. Thus, I also provide a sensitivity analysis on various WACCs on the equity value.

Cost of debt: 4.25%

Risk-free rate: 4.06%

Tax rate: 17%

Cost of debt (after tax) = 4.25% * (1-17%) = 3.53%

Beta: 1.16

Market risk premium: 4.33%

Cost of equity = Rfr + Beta x MRP = 9.08%

Equity: $145,315

Debt: $10,453

WACC: 8.71%

Valuation

The valuation comes out to be $384.51 for the perpetuity method and $399.34 using the Exit EBITDA method, where I used an EBITDA multiple of 22.0x.

Sensitivity Analysis

The following table shows a sensitivity analysis for the equity range. I am long here, and I believe this can hit $400.

Technical Analysis

This chart shows the cyclical moments in the price action, inspired by the concept of Hurst cycle analysis. The daily chart structure is really strong. There was this mid-July-August decline, but that too seems to be in a recovery phase now. But if you notice the Chaikin Money Flow (CMF) indicator (the green area indicator), there wasn't any institutional-level buying in that month.

However, recently the CMF has started to surge up, indicating institutional-level buying pressure. This could be due to recent announcements of a partnership with Nvidia, as I articulated earlier. A $400 December price target seems possible.

Bear Case

My entire premise is on the ramping up of AI infrastructure. If we hear surprise cuts to capex from the hyperscalers, then I expect Eaton to underperform. Additionally, Eaton's growth is tied to this electrification boom in the U.S., and any slowdown here will be bearish for Eaton. The HVDC infrastructure upgrade is not a plug-and-play type of solution. This is a multiyear (4+ years) theme and is tied to regulatory oversights and approvals. Eaton's growth rate can be severely restricted if these themes do not play out.

Eaton's margin growth depends on the Electrify Global segment. Turnaround plays are always challenging, and if Eaton's new team is not able to turn around the global segment, investors might lose confidence.

Conclusion

Eaton's prior quarter was strong. The company is positive for the remaining quarters of this fiscal year, and management has already provided insights that the book-to-bill is above 1. There is a huge backlog in the data center industry in the U.S. and Europe that should provide confidence to management and investors for Eaton's revenue growth in the next 3-5 years. Further, the auto industry seems to be recovering, which should act as a nice tailwind for Eaton.

The perpetuity method indicated a fair value of approximately $384.51, and the exit EBITDA method indicated a fair value of approximately $399.34 at a 22.0x multiple. Technical analysis indicates a price target of $400 by December is feasible.

With the electrification boom in the U.S., I believe Eaton is a nice indirect AI data center play, and I rate the stock a Buy.

data.title: PENN Entertainment's Breakout Imminent - Bottom-Line Reversal Is Near
data.description:
PENN Continues To Offer A Compelling Inflection Story Despite The Recent Recovery

We previously covered PENN Entertainment (NASDAQ:PENN) in March 2025, discussing how its minimal growth profile and yet-to-bear-fruit ESPN Bet had already triggered the great pessimism observed in its stock prices and valuations.

Despite the mixed near-term prospects, the Interactive segment's ongoing cash burn, and the deteriorating balance sheet, we believed that the correction had triggered the rich triple-digit upside potential, assuming that the gaming company delivered improved bottom-lines as expected, aided by the emerging bullish uptrend support since the April 2024 bottom.

PENN 1Y Stock Price

Since that reiterated Buy rating, PENN has found a trading floor in the $13s range during the April 2025 meltdown before rallying by +42.7% by the time of writing, with a similar recovery also observed in its gaming peers in varying degrees.

Growing iGaming & Online Sports Betting Revenues

Much of their tailwinds are attributed to the growing iGaming and Online Sports Betting [OSB] revenues in the US, with the online spending share of the overall US gaming revenues already expanding to 33.5% by Q2'25, nearly tripling compared to the 13% reported in Q2'21.

The same robust growth has been observed in the higher iGaming and OSB revenues of $6.52B in Q2'25 (+27% YoY), with June 2025 bringing forth even richer growth profile for iGaming at +31.1% YoY and sports betting at +39.5% YoY to an overall monthly revenue of $6.4B.

These paint a promising picture indeed, with it naturally underscoring why PENN along with its gaming peers have been able to report robust stock price recovery trends thus far.

This is significantly aided by its media partner's, Disney's (DIS) decision to unify the existing Disney+ offerings with Hulu and ESPN D2C offerings from August 2025 onwards, significantly aided by the expanded partnership with NFL/ WWE.

This development may very well allow PENN to gain an advantage in the unified D2C platform, thanks to the strategic positioning that ESPN Bet may enjoy from the easier user acquisition channels and the expanded sports betting offerings.

If anything, readers may want to note that eMarketer already expects more US sports viewers to choose the digital platform moving forward, with 2027 expected to bring forth 127.4M of viewers in the US (expanding at a 3Y CAGR of +6.5% from 2024 levels of 105.3M), at the expense of the declining traditional pay TV viewers from 85.4M in 2024 to 75.4M in 2027.

These reasons may perhaps explain why more media companies have expanded on their sports content thus far, with it triggering PENN's robust long-term prospects as a gaming platform as 55% of the American adults also participated in gambling activities in 2024, compared to the 43% reported in 2019.

As a result, we can understand why PENN has reported robust Interactive revenue growth to $316.1M in FQ2'25 (+8.9% QoQ/ +35.8% YoY) and a narrowing cash burn at adj EBITDAR margin of -19.6% (+11 points QoQ/ +24.5 YoY).

This is while guiding further Interactive adj EBITDA improvement to -$55M in FQ3'25 (+11.2% QoQ/ +39.4% YoY) and $5M in FQ4'25 (+109% QoQ/ +104% YoY), with it underscoring the ongoing inflection in its iGaming and OSB execution.

These developments perhaps underscore why PENN has felt confident enough to guide the Interactive segment to be profitable for the full year of 2026, allowing them to drive profitable growth from FQ4'25 onwards.

This is while building upon the retail property level revenues of $1.4B (inline QoQ/ YoY) and adj EBITDAR margin of 33.8% in FQ2'25 (+0.7 points QoQ/ inline YoY), with these naturally being accretive to the overall company's top/ bottom-line prospects from FY2026 onwards.

The Consensus Forward Estimates

These reasons are also why the consensus forward estimates remain promising, with PENN expected to drive adj EPS inflection from FY2025 onwards as the Interactive segment becomes accretive to its overall prospects.

PENN Valuations

This reason may also be why the market has already upgraded PENN's FWD P/E non-GAAP valuations to 62.86x, up from the 1Y mean of 36.99x, the 5Y mean of 31.87x, and the sector median of 18.14x.

For now, it is apparent that the stock is rather expensive compared to its gaming peers, including:

  • DraftKings (DKNG) at FWD P/E non-GAAP valuations to 26.36x and the 2Y mean of 36.57x and
  • Flutter Entertainment (FLUT) at 32.53x/ 32.35x, respectively.

Even so, we are not overly concerned, since PENN remains somewhat reasonable at FY2027 P/E non-GAAP valuations of 7.41x, based on the current share price of $18.91 and the consensus FY2027 adj EPS estimates of $2.55, with it apparent that its ongoing bottom-line inflection remains compellingly valued despite the high double-digit rally from the April 2025 bottom.

So, Is PENN Stock A Buy, Sell, or Hold?

PENN 4Y Stock Price

For now, PENN has had a notable uptrend support line since the April 2025 bottom, thanks to the promising industry development and the company's excellent performance metrics, significantly aided by the Fed's recent pivot where consumer discretionary spending is likely to remain robust.

PENN's Likely To Be Durable Upward Momentum

PENN's technical indicators also suggest its likely to be durable upward momentum, with the reasonable Relative Strength Index [RSI] and the low Trading Volumes implying its ability to break out of the prior sideways trading cadence since early 2024.

This conjecture is not overly aggressive as well, with there remaining an excellent more than doubling upside potential to our bull-case long-term price target of $81.20 based on the 5Y P/E mean of 31.87x (near to the sector mean of ~34x) and the consensus FY2027 adj EPS estimates of $2.55.

Thanks to the rich capital appreciation prospect, the seemingly bullish uptrend support, the exuberant market sentiments/ likely to be improved spending trends arising from the Fed's recent rate cut, and the recent bounce at the $18s ranges by September 15, 2025, we are reiterating our Buy rating for the PENN stock here.
